content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
September 29, 1976

Mr. Thomas G. Redmon
Wilke, Fleury, Hoffelt & Gray
Downtown Plaza Towers, Suite 1100
555 Capitol Mall
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Redmon:

This letter responds to your opinion request of September 17, 1976, concerning reporting by a lobbyist employer. It is my understanding that you have submitted your letter should be treated as a request for written advice, submitted pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18329, instead of a formal opinion request.

Based on the facts stated in your letter and additional facts disclosed to a member of the staff, Merck, Sharp and Dohme is not required to disclose sales of pharmaceuticals to pharmacies which are also agency officials with the California Department of Health. As a lobbyist employer, Merck, Sharp and Dohme must report "exchanges" with business entities in which the filer knows or has reason to know that an agency official is a "proprietor, partner, director, officer, manager, or has more than a fifty percent ownership interest" if the total value of such exchanges is one thousand dollars or more in a calendar year. Government Code Section 86109(e). The Commission has adopted regulations interpreting the term "exchange" which provides, in relevant part, that the term does not include transactions with agency officials or their business entities resulting from offers of goods or services made by a filer if such offers are made on identical terms to the public at large. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18650(b)(1). You have stated that the pharmacists purchase goods from Merck, Sharp and Dohme on the basis of a pharmaceutical catalog which lists the price for each drug and that the price paid by all pharmacists is the price listed in the catalog. These transactions appear to fall within the exclusion set forth above, because the goods are offered to all potential purchasers at the same price. Thus, Merck, Sharp and Dohme's sales are not "exchanges" within the meaning of the Political Reform Act and are not required to be reported.

I hope this information will be of assistance. If you have any questions, please contact Natalie West, a staff attorney, at 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Michael Bennett
Executive Director

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 • EXECUTIVE/LEGAL 322-5901 • ENFORCEMENT/CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6441

The image appears to be nearly blank or extremely faded, with only a few small circular marks visible at the top of the page. I cannot make out any legible text to transcribe.

LAW OFFICES

WILKE, FLEURY, HOFFELT & GRAY

SHERMAN C. WILKE
GORDON A. FLEURY
RICHARD N. HOFFELT
JOE S. GRAY
WILLIAM A. GOULD, JR.
PHILIP R. BIRNEY
THOMAS G. REDMON
ROBERT L. FREEWAY
DONALD REX HECKMAN II
ALAN G. PERKINS

DOWNTOWN PLAZA TOWERS
SUITE 1100
555 CAPITOL MALL
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814

TELEPHONE
(916) 441-2430

September 17, 1976

PAUL PEEK
OF COUNSEL

Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Attention: Ms. Nan Hamilton
Technical Compliance Branch

Re: Request for Opinion

Dear Ms. Hamilton:

This opinion request is based upon the following
recitals:

1. It is made pursuant to the provisions of
Section 18320 of Division 6 of Title II of the Administrative Code.

2. It is made by Merck, Sharp & Dohme, a pharmaceutical manufacturing company, whose duties, if any,
under the Political Reform Act of 1974 are in question.

3. It presents a question of common concern to
all pharmaceutical manufacturers who make direct product
sales to pharmacies within this State.

4. The question presented is one which you and I
have discussed previously by telephone, as more fully
explained below.

The question upon which your opinion is requested
is as follows:

DOES SECTION 86109(e) OF THE POLITICAL REFORM ACT
OF 1974 REQUIRE A PHARMACEUTICAL MANUFACTURING COMPANY TO
GATHER, COLLATE AND REPORT THE MULTITUDE OF TRANSACTIONS
WHICH THE COMPANY MAY HAVE IN THE CUSTOMARY AND USUAL
COURSE OF BUSINESS WITH A WHOLESALE OR RETAIL PHARMACY IN
WHICH AN ELECTIVE STATE, LEGISLATIVE OR AGENCY OFFICIAL OR
STATE CANDIDATE HAS A SUBSTANTIAL INTEREST?

Fair Political Practices Commission
September 17, 1976
Page 2


The above question and request for an opinion is
based upon the factual situation stated immediately below.

Merck, Sharp & Dohme manufactures and distri-
butes a considerable number of pharmaceutical and biologi-
cal products for both human and veterinary use. It is a
foreign corporation, although it has branch offices in
Los Angeles and San Francisco. Overall, the company has
in the neighborhood of 5,000 to 6,000 retail and wholesale
customers in the State of California.

The State of California, and in particular the
Department of Health, is the employer of numerous pharma-
cists. Many of these pharmacists continue to maintain
management or proprietary interests in local pharmacies.
Many State agencies also maintain Boards (such as the
Medical Therapeutic and Drug Advisory Committee of the
Department of Health) to which pharmacists and pharmacy
owners have been appointed. Considering these individuals
in tandem with the elective and legislative officials,
both present and prospective, who may have a substantial
interest in a pharmacy, the number of wholesale and re-
tail accounts which might be held subject to the reporting
requirements of Section 86109(e) is substantial.

Merck, Sharp & Dohme sells its products inter-
nationally, as well as in every state of the union. It
has no way of monitoring on a uniform basis the indivi-
als specified in Section 86109(e) or of monitoring changes
in ownership. Because of this and the considerable number
of wholesale and retail accounts of the company, it would
be extremely burdensome, if not virtually impossible, to
set up a reporting system for such transactions. It would
be necessary to run customer lists against a list of indi-
viduals named in Section 86109(e). The resulting list,
which could be immense, would then form a basis for fur-
ther reports which could in themselves be substantially
valueless because of their size, if for no other reason.

The sheer number of individual transactions
which a typical pharmacy has with a major pharmaceutical
manufacturing company also makes reporting both burdensome
and useless. Based on the size of the pharmacy stockroom
and the ability of the pharmacy to anticipate needs, the

Fair Political Practices Commission
September 17, 1976
Page 3

pharmacy may enter into a number of transactions with the
same company on the same day. The transactions may range
from the specific need for one bottle of a rarely-used
product to a large order for a month's supply of a commonly used product. Thus, the number of transactions
which could potentially be held reportable under Section
86109(e) could be overwhelming. The preparation and review of such reports would seem to be a waste of time and
effort and the information derived would not seem to be
in the direction of the stated purpose of the Fair Political Practices Act.

Furthermore, the possibility of abuse in this
area is minimal. Sales to wholesale and retail pharmacies are made on the basis of published catalog prices
and in accordance with established company procedures
which make no distinctions based upon the political status
of the business entity involved. Billing is handled
mechanically, on the basis of published and generally
available catalog prices. For the reasons set forth above,
it is a feeling of the company that it is not within the
intent and purpose of the Political Reform Act to require
the reporting of business transactions exceeding $1,000.00
as long as such transactions are in the customary and
usual course of business.

As you may recall, we discussed this specific
matter by telephone in the recent past. At that time you
told me that the reporting problems faced by Merck, Sharp
& Dohme are common to a number of other business entities
who have voiced similar objections. At that time you
told me that the Commission intended to seek amendment
to the Political Reform Act to exempt business entities
such as Merck, Sharp & Dohme from the owners' reporting
requirements of transactions made in the usual and customary course of business. You further informed me that an
opinion request pursuant to Section 18320 of Division 6
of Title II of the Administrative Code would be held, but
not answered until the requisite legislative changes had
been made. Pursuant to this understanding, I will not
expect a response to this letter request until a legislative solution has been reached.

Fair Political Practices Commission
September 17, 1976
Page 4


Thanking you in advance for your cooperation,
I remain

Very truly yours,

WILKE, FLEURY, HOFFELT & GRAY

By [signature]
THOMAS G. REDMON

TGR:sb
cc: Merck, Sharp & Dohme
     Attn: Mr. William H. Culviner

I can see this image is almost entirely blank/white with only a few very faint marks at the top that are difficult to make out clearly. There appear to be some light handwritten marks or notations at the top of the page, but they are too faint and unclear for me to transcribe with confidence.

If you need me to transcribe the content of this document, could you please provide a clearer or higher contrast version of the image?

Redmon of Wilke, Fleury, Hoffelt & Gray in Sacramento, California. The letter explains that Merck, Sharp and Dohme is not required to disclose sales of pharmaceuticals to pharmacists who are also agency officials with the California Department of Health. As a lobbyist employer, Merck, Sharp and Dohme must report "exchanges" with business entities if the total value of such exchanges is one thousand dollars or more in a calendar year. The term "exchange" does not include transactions with agency officials or their business entities resulting from offers of goods or services made by a filer if such offers are made on identical terms to the public at large. Mr. Redmon had stated that pharmacists purchase goods from Merck, Sharp and Dohme on the basis of a pharmaceutical catalog which lists the price for each drug and that the price paid by all pharmacists is the price listed in the catalog. These transactions appear to fall within the exclusion set forth above, because the goods are offered to all potential purchasers at the same price. Thus, Merck, Sharp and Dohme's sales are not "exchanges" within the meaning of the Political Reform Act and are not required to be reported. The Commission has adopted regulations interpreting the term "exchange" which provides, in relevant part, that the term does not include transactions with agency officials or their business entities resulting from offers of goods or services made by a filer if such offers are made on identical terms to the public at large. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18650(b)(1). The Commission advises Mr. Redmon that this information will be of assistance. If he has any questions, please contact Natalie West, a staff attorney, at 322-5901.
